item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably necroposting, but the amount of useless or unneeded [DATA EXPUNGED]s is pretty annoying. Especially on that first exploration log, it just feels like the author didnt bother to write it, so they just expunged large quantities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last log is oddly placed, it could be moved without problems and it would actually help the scip. There's something wrong with this one, I don't really know what it is. Sorry, downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it eats our failures and if you think your life is doomed go be their dinner so you dont feel the pain.
…sheesh. Dark minds much, 1734?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, guess it's just me who hasn't heard it in the maritime community… But I am Canadian, so perhaps its a regional thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of those time where wikipedia has it wrong.
While "doldrums" is often used, and certainly originated, colloquially; it is a valid and technical term for the phenomenon.

There is always the chance I am mistaken on this. But if I am, so are sea-faring men of my acquaintance spanning three generations. Including two retired US Navy officers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I use "doldrums" twice, once by ship's captain (which makes sense), and once in a researcher's note, where it doesn't make sense, but notes are considered to be less strict on tone in my headcanon, and I can't find a practical alternative to the word "doldrums".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would suggest that the word "doldrum" should be annotated or replaced, as it is a maritime colloquialism rather than a commonly known or technical term.

Other than that, fantastic article.

item.title: Oh you were finished? Then allow me to retort.
item.content:encoded: yes.avi

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope.avi

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't help but believe that 1734 is a adult version of 252.

item.title: DOCTOR ANBOROUGH NOT DOING KETER? HOW IS THIS POSSIBLE?
item.content:encoded: Take my upvote. Another brilliant piece of work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The supplement is finally posted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now all of my articles have pictures…it took forever to find a usable one for this article, since the whole "no signs of deterioration" thing meant I couldn't use a shipwreck picture (and I can't use photoshop), still, I think this works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, maybe add an addendum/whatever after the scientist's note, indicating that subjective perception of certain specific sounds in the area is inconsistent with objective instrumental measurements (there are sounds that are "objectively" at n dBA but you "experience" them as if they were n-50% dBA)?

Just a thought

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The sand and water does not come within 1 cm either. I suppose you could say it is floating, but only by 1 centimeter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely seconded, but can't quite put my finger on why except for "less information is more scary". That addendum was the part that actually felt chilling, but expanding it would only hurt the effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Direct contact with the structure of the vessel appears impossible: matter cannot come closer than 1 cm of the walls, floor, ceiling, and supports.

So does the ship float 1 cm above the beach? Or does the repulsion not affect the sand?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I made some slight modifications to the journal and researcher's note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, realistically, the D-Class aren't aware of the meta-universe, or the unfortunate ends of the D-Class in the other logs, and are just normal humans put into a situation that's scary as fuck…so it's a reasonable reaction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The problem is that it's so common in every D-Class log that it stretches suspension of disbelief. It's like he's swearing just for the purpose of swearing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The dialogue struck me as a little bit stilted, but not so much as to pull me out of the article. In any case, the amount of profanity wasn't the problem. If I were forced to dive into a twilight deep ocean dimension filled with disembodied tentacles and corpse-using decorator crab abominations, I'd be cussing up a storm too. Well, I'd probably be terrified past the ability to form coherent words, but if I could I would be cussing like a D-class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've heard people cuss more gratuitously than the D-Class in a casual conversation. That might sound fucked up but it's true, so I wouldn't say this dialogue is unrealistic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My brother and I say "for-fucking-ever" with some frequency. I've heard it at least once from a friend as well (likely because we use it so often).

If it really grates when someone else reads it I can't comment on that, but to me and my twisted vocabulary it sounds fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The information about the corpses and the source of the tentacles is incomplete, I would say it's out-of-character for the Foundation to include that in the official segment of the report until they have a better idea of what's going on. SCP-1983, which was a definite influence on this, does something similar.

The D-Class isn't swearing because of prison, but more because he's being forced down a hole with a giant tentacle sticking out of it and he's freaking out.

For-fucking-ever is used by Lewis Black; sorry, that's there just because I really wanted to use the line.

I appreciate your comments and I will think on if and how I want to edit the content here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea was alright, but it really didn't do too much for me, so I was originally going to no vote this. However, the technical issues with this ended up tipping me over to a downvote:

  • Your grammar and tone needs some touching up in places.
  • You have a bad case of 'all D-Class swear like sailors'. Going to prison doesn't automatically make one the most obnoxious person and colorfully vulgar person. Do you know many people who use 'for-fucking-ever'?
  • I dislike your expungements and you use it unnecessarily in some places, such as the cutting off of the air. Why was that expunged? It's not like we don't know morally ambiguous shit happens, and honestly that would probably cause more of a problem IC than if it weren't expunged.
  • Both of your notes have really strange single sentence paragraphs. I get you're attempting to do this for dramatic flair, but this really stands out in the journal entries. For me, single paragraph entries already push it somewhat, but you wouldn't put a context-less sentence such as 'It's getting dark' in a journal without some sort of segue or more reason for saying it, such as 'so I should go to bed'.
  • If they see corpses being reanimated in the log, shouldn't that be mentioned somewhere in the description? Is that part of the expungement? Why is shown if it is?
  • This isn't so much of a complaint as it is advice: You could probably do with sticking those parentheses into footnotes.

I may switch my vote back to a neutral vote if you address some of these, but as it is I'm gonna have to stick with a downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They searched the island extensively but found absolutely nothing; there is sound, but the sound of the water just seems unusually quiet. I know they have reason to suspect it, but they can't call it an anomaly if they can't find any reason to indicate it's anomalous.

The tentacle cannot reach any fish. It extends out and onto the deck, but doesn't make it into the ocean around the ship. The submerged space within the anomaly (the place that the unfortunate D-Class drops into) is not in the ocean around the ship, it's in the "home".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I may be wrong, but if the Foundation knew that the island was suspect, they had evidence that the island had an egg on it, they did not locate any parents, and then they just leave it alone? Especially with that last note saying that the lack of sound (seagulls). It seems a bit out of character.
Also, is there any mention of the fish nearby, or lack thereof? Does the tentacle thing eat fish, dolphins, sharks, shrimp, or just humans? I like this topic, but it seems almost unscientific to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The possible explanations I come up with are quite tentacular; I can't quite put my finger on which is correct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No need to rush, is there? You're well in the positives, and most of the stuff on your to-do list is beneficial but not fundamental to the article. Take your time, the wiki will still be here!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ack. I'll try to fix it up a little, but I don't know how fast I can have it ready. I have to be really careful about how I edit it at this point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, now I'm going to be that pissy guy who asks you to change something, then complains when you do it.

The additions are pretty straightforward. Too much so. I understand the narrative better now, but it sort feels like it got spoon fed to me …

I know, I'm a demanding prima donna. :P

Make us work a bit more for that info. Also, it seems odd that the thing is able to speak to the captain in the end. It's not mentioned to communicate with people anywhere else, and it feels like that happens just so we can get the "omg eaten forever!" note. Could you change it so that the captured people sort of call out somehow? I liked the tentacle better when it was just an alien thing.

In summary, I like that we're given more info, but it might work a little better if that info is delivered in a more atmospheric way. I hope you keep working on it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was wavering a bit on this but I'm a sucker for notes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't speak for him, of course, but the last line about the ocean being quiet is a bit off-tone. Not enough to kill it for me, but sort of off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I made the modifications the the captain's journal. I'm really kind of nervous about these changes, but I hope it will tie the article together better. Let me know what you guys think, and if the article's worse or better with the new information.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm looking forward to it. I agree on your priorities, too. Getting the language right increases immersion, but that won't help if the material isn't there in the first place.

I share your unease about the deep ocean and tentacular things that dwell there, so I'll definitely be back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the link, I'm going to make some modifications to the journal soon and this will be madly useful.

The modifications to the journal should tie the ship, the…thing, and the second island together better. I'm going to post the new material before modifying the whole thing to have proper 18th Century syntax, since the material itself is the more pressing issue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know. The second island is honestly the creepiest part of the article to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is my problem with the article, too. I don't know whether to contemplate the stasis-ship, the doldrums area, the tentacle, or what. If I had some idea how they connect to each other by the end it might work, but instead I'm too confused to be creeped out. Perhaps a few more hints would help me conjure up an appropriately worrying mental image of what in the hell is going on here.

Also, minor nitpick, the final log doesn't read like an 18th century log to me. It's no big deal, but for reference this is a few pages excerpted from the logbook of the Bonhomme Richard ca. 1782.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's an awful lot going on in here: temporally locked ship (saying that outright is kind of not tonally good, methinks), spatial anomaly, freaky tentacle. I didn't like it until the source of the tentacle was described; prior to that, this sounds like a goodly number of video game bosses. The journal feels negated by the researcher's note, and that by itself doesn't add anything. I mean, it's kind of surprising that a researcher would say, "Nah, we don't need to explore this". If there was something else going on here, that could be used, but it would also begin to stretch this article into too many areas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the first article where I actually sat down and thought about what scared me before writing it; the ocean is eerie to me, and tentacles (mainly the suckers) scare the crap out of me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm considering removing the mention of the second island in the note. The doldrums will still be there, but the note will now say that they didn't find any other islands in the area at all (but the captain will still mention that they escaped from that "place" in his journal). The unsettling feelings would be describing the doldrums instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We need more about the other island and/or doldrums. They provide a good bit of intrigue and interest, but at the current detail level they are irrevelant asides that don't really fit with an official, anomaly-focused document.

Some hint or speculation as to why Island 2 was so unsettling would be sufficient. Doesn't need to explain anything, just enough to provide a data point to justify even mentioning it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still skeptical about the logs, but it's a pretty good effort. Upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why so? About the researcher's note, I mean.

item.title: Re: RELEASE THE KRAKEN
item.content:encoded:
Fixed the errors. My typos are usually that I forgot to put in a word like "the", they're usually not spelling errors, so I was kinda shocked that I missed that. Also after having to type 7569 30+ times I'm not really surprised that jumbled the numbers once. I'll think about removing the word "entity" in the log and footnote, or describe as an entity earlier in the article.

Also regarding the "floating". Technically, yes, you could say they're floating 1 cm over the deck. They are still "on" the ship, but they're not actually making contact with it. Also, it says that this only applies to the ships structure; any artifacts that are unattached to the structure, like the journal, are not temporally isolated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like more could be done with this. No idea what, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The researcher's note just kind of kills it for me. No vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. You've got some interesting ideas, but this could have been executed much better. No vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The island is not in the vicinity of any practical sailing route, as such, any civilians that come within 500 meters of the coast are considered to be suspect

I think you mean, "and as such," or something similar. You could also just shorten "considered to be suspect" to "considered suspect." Of course, the Foundation should also remove civilians encroaching on the containment site, but that's not the point.

item.title: RELEASE THE KRAKEN
item.content:encoded:
matter cannot come closer than 1 cm of the walls, floor, ceiling, and supports.

So… the explorator actually floated over the wreck?

D-7569 is a 37-year old Caucasian male with a height of 90 cm

He's rather short, isn't he?

Video feed confirms D-7569's claim, the tentacle appears to be protruding from a mass of human remains, approximately 20 meteres

Spelling error.

D-5769 starts screaming, approximately 10 meters above the mass.

So, who's that? :)

which indicate that D-7569 is entrapped by the entity's tentacles.

Sounds somehow wrong to me as you haven't noticed an entity up to this point.

This is an excerpt from a journal in the possession of the ship's captain

Was this found on the island or somewhere within the ship? Because you didn't mention any bodies and I would think the journal would be impossible to remove if it "belonged" tho the wreck because of the barrier (see above).

Also, I would recommend giving the vessel a nice and spooky name and adding more details about the anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of agree that it needs some tone touch-ups, but I think this definitely has potential. Just some things I noticed on first read through - D-7569 is 90cm, "multiple convictions of [DATA EXPUNGED]" seems unnecessary to me, as does "D-7569: "Please, what a-[IRRELEVANT DIALOGUE REDACTED]".
I think you need to meat the scip out a bit more, as overall we only get a small paragraph on what the SCP actually does. I know more about that weird island than I do about the actual SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you; would you mind if I ask what is errant in the log?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't entirely like the tone of the exploration log but I do like the concept enough for an upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Somehow I knew it was Dr. Bright's vote when I saw the downvote :)
